topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the page. The words swam before her eyes, familiar yet tainted by a sickening realization. Every carefully crafted sentence, every nuanced character arc she had poured her soul into, was mirrored, almost perfectly, in the latest release from Silas Thorne. A cold knot tightened in her stomach, spreading outward until her hands trembled, causing the lukewarm tea in her mug to slosh over the rim. She reread the passage, the phantom weight of someone else’s theft pressing down on her chest. A deep, shaky breath escaped her lips, and she pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. She walked to the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color, wishing she could simply dissolve into the glass.

[story 2]

"It’s just… impossible," Marcus muttered, running a hand repeatedly through his already disheveled hair. He leaned back in his worn armchair, the springs groaning under his weight. The manuscript comparison lay open on his lap, the glaring similarities between his unpublished novella and Thorne’s new bestseller a physical ache behind his eyes. He picked up his phone, his thumb hovering over his contact list before dropping it onto the cushion beside him with a soft thud. His jaw clenched so tightly that a dull throb radiated across his molars. He sighed, a long, ragged sound that seemed to carry all the weariness of weeks spent wrestling with this unwelcome truth. He just wanted the noise of the world to stop, to let the silence consume him.

[story 3]

The silence in the apartment was oppressive, thick enough to choke on. Clara traced the lines of the printed pages with a fingertip, the ink feeling like a venomous substance under her touch. Each shared phrase, each distinctive metaphor she had painstakingly developed, was now undeniably belonging to another. Her shoulders slumped, a heavy, defeated weight settling onto her spine. She stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing an escape from the suffocating proximity of the evidence. Her pace was uneven, jerky, as if her limbs were disconnected from her will. She paced the length of the living room three times, stopping only to press the heels of her palms against her eyes, trying to scrub the image of the copied words from her vision.
